methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $335M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy

data.narrative:
Tokamak Energy is a Series C nuclear energy company founded by David Kingham and Mikhail Gryaznevich that has raised $335M+ to date. The valuation analysis draws on a broader nuclear energy comp universe of 3 public companies (OKLO, NNE, SMR) and 8 private peers including Oklo, TerraPower, X-Energy, Kairos Power, and Nano Nuclear Energy.

Stage-progression methodology is the primary applicable valuation approach, using total capital raised ($335M) as a proxy base and applying a 1.7x step-up multiplier typical for Series C to next-stage advancement, yielding an implied valuation of $570M. Peer-median benchmarking could not be reliably applied: while 8 same-sector peers exist, none of the narrower filters—same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-tier matches—returned three or more datapoints sufficient for robust median comparison. Broadening to all-sector medians would introduce unacceptable noise.

The triangulated range is $484M (low), $570M (median), to $655M (high), derived from stage-progression analysis and sensitivity bounds around the 1.7x multiplier. The primary uncertainty remains the absence of sufficient narrowly comparable private precedents at Series C in nuclear energy, which limits confidence in peer validation. The range reflects stage-typical valuation steps but should be stress-tested against near-term funding conditions and capital intensity trajectories in frontier fission.
